quiz.title: SAT: Unit 2 Quiz 8
quiz.subtitle: Identifying Evidence That Supports or Undermines an Argument (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation

questions.question:
Climate scientists claim that Arctic sea-ice loss accelerates regional warming primarily by reducing surface albedo (reflectivity), not by releasing stored methane from thawing permafrost.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Year} & \text{Sea-Ice Extent (million km²)} & \text{Surface Albedo} \\ \hline 2000 & 6.2 & 0.68 \\ \hline 2010 & 4.9 & 0.54 \\ \hline 2020 & 3.8 & 0.41 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this claim?
options: Methane concentrations in Arctic air samples increased by 15% over the same observation period.
Regional temperature records show warming trends that correlate with global CO₂ levels.
Permafrost thaw rates accelerated significantly following the warmest summer on record.
Surface albedo declines in parallel with sea-ice extent, while methane emissions remain stable.
questions.explanation: Choice D directly supports the albedo mechanism by showing parallel declines in ice and reflectivity while holding methane constant. Choice A introduces methane increases, which would weaken the claim. Choice B shifts focus to global CO₂, irrelevant to the regional albedo mechanism. Choice C emphasizes permafrost thaw, the alternative mechanism the claim downplays.

questions.question:
Educational psychologists propose that growth-mindset interventions improve academic performance primarily by increasing student persistence after failure, not by enhancing initial motivation to begin tasks.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Group} & \text{Task Initiation Rate (%)} & \text{Persistence After Failure (%)} \\ \hline\text{Control} & 78 & 34 \\ \hline\text{Growth-Mindset} & 81 & 67 \\ \hline\text{Praise-Only} & 92 & 29 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this proposal?
options: Growth-mindset participants showed greater improvement on tasks requiring repeated attempts.
Students in the praise-only group achieved higher final grades despite lower persistence scores.
Task initiation rates increased modestly across all intervention groups compared to control.
Persistence scores predicted final performance more strongly than initiation rates in regression models.
questions.explanation: Choice B challenges the persistence mechanism by showing superior outcomes without high persistence, suggesting an alternative pathway. Choice A directly supports the proposed mechanism. Choice C addresses initiation, which the proposal explicitly downplays as secondary. Choice D provides correlational evidence favoring persistence, strengthening the claim.

questions.question:
Public health researchers claim that sugar-sweetened beverage taxes reduce obesity rates primarily by decreasing consumption among high-intake households, not by prompting reformulation of products by manufacturers.

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this claim?
options: Major beverage manufacturers reformulated 40% of taxed products to contain less sugar within two years.
Households in the top quartile of baseline beverage consumption reduced purchases by 31% following tax implementation.
Overall calorie intake declined equally across all income groups after the tax took effect.
Consumer awareness of the tax correlated positively with self-reported intentions to reduce sugary drinks.
questions.explanation: Choice B directly supports the consumption-reduction mechanism by showing targeted declines among high-intake households. Choice A emphasizes manufacturer reformulation, the alternative mechanism the claim downplays. Choice C suggests uniform effects across groups, inconsistent with targeted high-intake impact. Choice D addresses intentions, not actual consumption or obesity outcomes.

questions.question:
Historians argue that the adoption of mechanical clocks in medieval Europe accelerated economic development primarily by enabling precise labor coordination, not by improving astronomical navigation.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{City} & \text{Public Clocks Installed} & \text{Guild Membership Growth (%)} \\ \hline\text{Florence} & 3 & 142 \\ \hline\text{Venice} & 5 & 189 \\ \hline\text{Bruges} & 2 & 98 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this argument?
options: Guild membership growth correlated strongly with the number of public clocks installed across cities.
Labor contracts in clock-adopting cities included more precise time-based wage specifications.
Astronomical navigation techniques improved significantly following the spread of mechanical timekeeping.
Cities with earlier clock adoption showed no advantage in maritime trade volume compared to later adopters.
questions.explanation: Choice D challenges the labor-coordination mechanism by showing no economic advantage in trade (a coordination-dependent activity), suggesting clocks affected other domains. Choice A directly supports the proposed correlation between clocks and economic organization. Choice B provides documentary evidence for time-based labor coordination. Choice C emphasizes navigation improvements, the alternative mechanism the argument downplays.

questions.question:
Neuroscientists hypothesize that sleep deprivation impairs decision-making primarily by reducing prefrontal cortex activity, not by increasing amygdala reactivity to emotional stimuli.

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this hypothesis?
options: Sleep-deprived participants showed heightened amygdala activity when viewing threatening facial expressions.
Reaction times slowed equally across neutral and emotional decision tasks following sleep restriction.
Prefrontal activation during complex choices declined 40% after sleep loss, while amygdala responses to emotional images remained unchanged.
Participants who napped before testing showed restored prefrontal activity but continued amygdala hyperreactivity.
questions.explanation: Choice C directly supports the prefrontal mechanism by showing reduced activation with stable amygdala responses, isolating the proposed variable. Choice A emphasizes amygdala changes, which would weaken the hypothesis. Choice B addresses reaction time, not the neural mechanism comparison. Choice D shows mixed results that don't clearly isolate either mechanism.

questions.question:
Urban economists claim that ride-sharing services reduce private car ownership primarily by providing convenient on-demand alternatives, not by lowering the total cost of urban transportation.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{City} & \text{Ride-Share Trips/Capita} & \text{Car Ownership Rate (%)} \\ \hline\text{City A} & 12 & 68 \\ \hline\text{City B} & 34 & 51 \\ \hline\text{City C} & 58 & 39 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this claim?
options: Ride-share usage correlated negatively with car ownership rates across all three cities studied.
Survey data indicated that convenience was the primary reason users cited for choosing ride-sharing.
Total household transportation spending declined significantly in cities with high ride-share adoption.
Public transit ridership remained stable in cities following the introduction of ride-sharing platforms.
questions.explanation: Choice C challenges the convenience mechanism by showing cost reductions alongside adoption, suggesting financial incentives may drive the effect. Choice A directly supports the proposed correlation between ride-share use and reduced ownership. Choice B provides self-reported evidence favoring convenience. Choice D addresses transit substitution, not the car-ownership mechanism.

questions.question:
Linguists propose that children acquire grammatical rules primarily through statistical learning from exposure to language patterns, not through innate linguistic constraints.

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this proposal?
options: Children exposed to artificial languages with consistent statistical patterns mastered grammar faster than those exposed to irregular patterns.
Infants as young as six months showed neural responses to grammatical violations in their native language.
Children with limited language exposure still acquired core grammatical structures by age five.
Cross-linguistic studies revealed universal patterns in the order children acquire grammatical morphemes.
questions.explanation: Choice A directly supports statistical learning by showing that pattern consistency accelerates acquisition, matching the proposed mechanism. Choice B emphasizes early neural sensitivity, which could support innate constraints. Choice C suggests acquisition despite limited exposure, favoring innate mechanisms. Choice D highlights universal acquisition sequences, consistent with innate linguistic frameworks.

questions.question:
Agricultural scientists claim that cover cropping improves soil health primarily by increasing microbial diversity, not by reducing erosion through physical ground cover.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Plot Treatment} & \text{Microbial Diversity Index} & \text{Soil Loss (tons/ha)} \\ \hline\text{No Cover Crop} & 2.1 & 8.4 \\ \hline\text{Legume Cover} & 4.7 & 3.2 \\ \hline\text{Grass Cover} & 3.9 & 2.1 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this claim?
options: Plots with grass cover showed the lowest soil loss despite moderate microbial diversity gains.
Microbial diversity increased more in legume plots than in grass plots across multiple soil types.
Microbial activity levels correlated strongly with improved nutrient cycling rates across all treatment plots.
Soil organic matter content rose significantly in all cover-crop treatments compared to bare soil.
questions.explanation: Choice A directly challenges the microbial mechanism by showing the best erosion outcome with only moderate microbial gains, suggesting physical cover—not microbes—may drive the benefit. Choice B emphasizes microbial differences, supporting the claim. Choice C provides correlational evidence favoring microbial benefits, which strengthens rather than weakens. Choice D addresses organic matter, a separate soil-health indicator unrelated to the microbial-vs-physical mechanism debate.

questions.question:
Behavioral economists hypothesize that default options influence consumer choices primarily by reducing decision effort, not by signaling social approval of the default option.

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this hypothesis?
options: Default options labeled as 'most popular' were chosen at higher rates than unlabeled defaults.
Consumers reported greater satisfaction with choices when defaults aligned with their stated preferences.
Participants selected default options more frequently when under time pressure, regardless of perceived social norms.
Changing the default option altered aggregate choices even when participants were explicitly informed of the change.
questions.explanation: Choice C directly supports the effort-reduction mechanism by showing increased default adoption under cognitive load, isolating decision effort from social signaling. Choice A emphasizes social approval cues, which would weaken the hypothesis. Choice B addresses preference alignment, not the mechanism of default influence. Choice D shows default effects persist with awareness, but doesn't isolate effort vs. signaling.

questions.question:
Marine ecologists claim that overfishing reduces reef resilience primarily by removing herbivorous fish that control algae, not by disrupting predator-prey balances among carnivorous species.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Fishing Pressure} & \text{Herbivore Biomass (kg/ha)} & \text{Algal Cover (%)} \\ \hline\text{Low} & 142 & 18 \\ \hline\text{Moderate} & 67 & 41 \\ \hline\text{High} & 23 & 79 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this claim?
options: Algal cover increased inversely with herbivore biomass across all three fishing-pressure categories.
Herbivorous fish species declined more steeply than carnivorous species following fishing intensification.
Experimental removal of predators led to mesopredator release and subsequent declines in herbivore populations.
Reefs with intact predator populations showed faster recovery from algal overgrowth even when herbivore biomass was low.
questions.explanation: Choice D challenges the herbivore mechanism by showing recovery possible via predator-mediated pathways even with low herbivores, suggesting an alternative resilience route. Choice A directly supports the proposed herbivore-algae relationship. Choice B emphasizes herbivore declines, consistent with the claim. Choice C describes a trophic cascade that ultimately affects herbivores, indirectly supporting the claim.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can accurately identify evidence that directly strengthens or weakens specific claims by evaluating mechanism alignment.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on whether the evidence addresses the exact mechanism stated in the claim, not just a related topic.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: eliminate options that are true but irrelevant, or that support a different aspect of the argument.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Ask yourself: does this finding prove or disprove the specific causal relationship the claim describes?
